title: Chapter 45: Dawn of War
content: The first light of dawn painted the zombie moat in shades of blood and amber.

Marcus stood on the Citadel's catwalk, watching the horde of the dead stand at silent attention. Three hundred and forty zombies, arranged in two concentric rings around New Eden's perimeter, their empty eyes fixed outward, their bodies swaying almost imperceptibly in the morning breeze like kelp in a current. They didn't moan. They didn't shuffle. They just... stood. Held in place by Sera's will, mediated through the Multi-Partner Bond so the effort didn't kill her.

Through the Bond, he could feel her concentration — a vast, slow tide of mental force that held the dead like a conductor holding an orchestra in readiness. She stood on the roof above him, her white hair streaming in the wind, her arms slightly raised, her eyes closed.

Below, New Eden's 87 survivors had taken their positions. Raj had the generator running at full capacity, powering the perimeter lights that would be useless in daylight but invaluable if the siege lasted past sundown. Rebecca coordinated the non-combatants in the community center, turning it into a field hospital annex. The dozen or so people who could shoot had been given rifles from the armory and positions along the walls.
It wasn't much. But the zombie moat changed everything.
The first raiders appeared on the eastern road at 6:47 AM.

They came in formation — three armored vehicles leading, followed by a convoy of modified trucks and technicals. Marcus counted through binoculars: forty vehicles, maybe a hundred and fifty fighters, moving with the confidence of an army that had never lost.
Until they saw the moat.

The lead vehicle — a reinforced Suburban with a mounted .50 cal — stopped a hundred yards from the inner ring. The gunner swiveled, trying to figure out what he was looking at. Zombies. A wall of zombies. Standing in perfect formation, not attacking, not moving, just... waiting.
"What the hell?" The gunner's voice crackled over the walkie-talkie, intercepted by Vivienne's shortwave array.
"Contact," Vivienne said calmly from the comms tower. "Lead element has stopped. Command vehicle is... Crane is getting out."

Marcus watched as Victor Crane stepped from the second vehicle. He was tall — six-two, military bearing, shaved head, tactical vest over civilian clothes. The look of a man who'd been dangerous before the apocalypse and had only gotten more so since.
He raised binoculars and studied the zombie moat for a long moment. Then he lowered them and said something Marcus couldn't hear — but Vivienne could.
"He's saying..." She paused, adjusting the frequency. "'They've got zombies on a leash. Impressive. Kill the leash, the dogs scatter.'"
Marcus's jaw tightened. "He's going to target Sera."
"Obviously." Vivienne's voice was dry. "Shall I tell her, or will the Bond handle that?"

The Bond had already handled it. Sera's awareness sharpened like a blade, and through the link Marcus felt her shift her weight — a subtle repositioning that made her a harder target from the eastern approach.
"Snipers," Sera said through the Bond. "Two. Water tower, and the crane on the construction site on Fifth. They're setting up."
"I see them," Junko said. She was crouched on the wall's southeast corner, her ember-red eyes tracking the sniper teams with predatory focus. "I will remove. Ten minutes."
"Take the water tower," Marcus said. "Sera, can you get the crane?"

"Already moving." Sera didn't step off the roof — she *flowed* off it, a white blur that dropped four stories and hit the ground running, the zombie moat parting around her like water around a ship's prow.
The first shots came ninety seconds later.

Not from the snipers. From the main raider line — a sustained barrage of automatic fire aimed at the zombie moat. The dead fell in droves. And kept falling. The line held because for every zombie that dropped, another shuffled forward to take its place, pulled from the surrounding suburbs by Sera's Queen Authority.
But the raiders were learning. They started aiming for heads.
"Marcus," Elena's voice came through the earpiece. "The moat is thinning on the western side. She can't pull them fast enough."
"Elena, what's our casualty count?"
"Zero. So far. But if they breach—"
"They won't."

He said it with more confidence than he felt. Through the Bond, he touched each of his partners briefly — Sera's fierce concentration, Junko's lethal joy, Elena's worried determination, Vivienne's analytical calm. The Multi-Partner Bond hummed with shared purpose.
Then the flank attack came.
Thirty raiders on foot, moving through the ruins of Maple Street, trying to circle around the western edge of the moat where it was thinnest. They thought they were being clever.
Junko was waiting.

She dropped from a rooftop onto the lead raider — a 180-pound man in tactical gear — and knocked him unconscious before his brain registered the impact. The second man raised his rifle. Junko disarmed him in three movements, stripped the weapon, and used it to crack the third man across the jaw. She wasn't killing them. She was *disarming* them — literally and figuratively — stripping their weapons and tossing them into the moat, leaving panicked, unarmed men running for their lives through a field of the dead.
"Holy—" one of them screamed, and then a zombie grabbed his ankle, and he made a sound that Marcus had never heard a human make before.
The zombie didn't bite. It just held him. Sera's control was absolute.
"RETREAT!" The raider sergeant's voice cracked on the word. "FALL BACK! THEY'RE USING THE DEAD AS—"
Vivienne cut in on the open frequency: "As what? A wall? A moat? A very unfriendly neighborhood watch? Honestly, gentlemen, you walked into this one."

East side, Sera had dealt with the snipers. The water tower was empty. The construction crane was... Marcus didn't want to know what had happened on the crane. Sera was back on the Citadel roof, breathing hard, her control over the moat absolute again.
By mid-morning, the assault had stalled. Crane's forces had lost the element of surprise, twelve men to Junko's flank defense, and their confidence. The zombie moat held. New Eden was untouched.
But Crane wasn't done.
Through the binoculars, Marcus watched the raider king assembling his elite — thirty of his best fighters, armored and armed, gathering around the command vehicle. Something big was coming.
"Vivienne," Marcus said. "What are they saying?"

Silence on the radio. Then: "They're switching to encrypted channels. I can't—" A burst of static. Then Vivienne's voice, very quiet: "Marcus. They're bringing up the rocket launcher. And Crane is going to lead the push personally."
The siege wasn't over. It was just getting started.

**[BATTLE STATUS: STALEMATE]**
**[New Eden Integrity: 100%]**
**[Zombie Moat: 72% sustained]**
**[Crane's Raiders: 60% effective]**
**[Sera's Concentration: 84% — sustainable for 6 more hours]**
**[Elena's Medical Bay: 0 casualties]**
**[Junko's Flank: 12 raiders disarmed, 0 killed]**
